title: Captions and Transcripts
learningObjectives: Distinguish between open captions, closed captions, and transcripts and explain when each is required
Identify why auto-generated captions alone do not satisfy WCAG requirements without human review
Select and use practical captioning tools available to faculty (YouTube, Otter.ai, Microsoft Stream, Zoom)

keyTerms.definition: Text that appears on-screen synchronized with video or audio content, including dialogue, sound descriptions, and speaker identification. Closed captions can typically be turned on or off by the viewer, unlike open captions which are always visible.

Text that is permanently displayed on-screen, burned into the video itself. Unlike closed captions, open captions cannot be turned off. They are useful for videos that will be viewed in noisy environments (airport terminals, gyms) but are less flexible than closed captions.

A complete, text-based version of the audio content in a video or audio file. A transcript includes all spoken dialogue, speaker identification, and may include descriptions of important visual or sound information. Transcripts are not synchronized with the video but provide full access to the content.

Technology that converts spoken audio to text automatically, without human intervention. ASR is fast and low-cost but often produces errors, especially with technical vocabulary, proper nouns, accents, and background noise. Auto-captions must be reviewed and edited by a human before they are accessible.

How accurately a caption reflects what was spoken. Industry standards (like DCMP's Accurate, Consistent, Clear, Readable, Equal) aim for 99%+ accuracy. Auto-captions typically achieve 80–90% accuracy, which is insufficient for educational content without human review.

Real-time text display of spoken content during a live event (a lecture, meeting, presentation). CART stands for Communication Access Realtime Translation and is provided by a trained stenographer. Live auto-captions (like Zoom's) are faster and lower-cost but less accurate.

contentBlocks.title: Captions, Transcripts, and the Law: When They Are Required
contentBlocks.body:
Under ADA Title II and WCAG, **captions and transcripts are legally required for pre-recorded audio and video content in course materials**. Knowing exactly when each is required is important for compliance and resource planning.

**Pre-Recorded Video with Sound**: Requires both captions and audio description (if visual information is essential).
- Captions must be synchronized with the video (they play at the same time)
- Transcripts should be available as an alternative format
- Example: A lecture recording, a science demonstration video, a documentary clip

**Pre-Recorded Audio Only**: Requires a transcript.
- A podcast, a recorded lecture, an audio file for language study
- Captions are not applicable (there is no video to synchronize with)
- A transcript provides the full text

**Live Presentation with Audio**: Requires captions provided in real-time (live captions).
- A synchronous Zoom lecture, a live webinar, a broadcast
- Captions must be provided simultaneously as the presentation happens
- Options: CART (a professional stenographer), live auto-captions (Zoom, Teams, etc.), or a captioner

**Interactive Media or Simulations**: Requires captions for any audio, plus keyboard accessibility.
- An H5P interactive lesson, a PhET simulation with audio narration, a Bozeman Science animation
- Audio must have captions, and the interactive element must be operable by keyboard

**Video with No Sound**: No captions required, but may need a description of what is happening.
- A silent film clip, a video of a physical process with no narration
- However, students should still understand what the video shows — consider adding narration or a text description

**Why "Auto-Captions Alone" Are Not Sufficient**

YouTube, Zoom, Teams, Otter.ai, and other platforms now offer automatic speech recognition (ASR) that generates captions in seconds. This is wonderful progress — it makes captioning accessible to everyone.

But here is the critical point: **auto-generated captions are a draft, not a final product**. They require human review and editing. Why?

- **Technical terminology**: "mitochondria" becomes "middle chondria," "photosynthesis" becomes "photo synthesis." ASR struggles with discipline-specific vocabulary.
- **Proper nouns and names**: "Jean-Jacques Rousseau" might appear as "Jean jaques rosso" or "gene jack rooseau." Names of people and places are frequently misheard.
- **Accents and speech patterns**: A professor with a Southern accent, a non-native English speaker, or someone who speaks quickly will have higher error rates. ASR is trained primarily on neutral, standard American English.
- **Homophones and context**: "There," "their," and "they're" all sound the same, but ASR might pick the wrong word. A speaker says "to implement the policy" but ASR writes "to implicate the policy."
- **Background noise**: A lecture recorded in a room with echoes, traffic outside, or students talking will have more errors than a clean studio recording.

**Industry Standard: DCMP Captioning Quality**

The [Described and Captioned Media Program (DCMP)](https://dcmp.org/learn/captioningkey/598) defines the gold standard for caption quality:

- **Accurate**: Captions match the spoken dialogue verbatim, including terminology, slang, and accented speech
- **Consistent**: Formatting, capitalization, and speaker identification are consistent throughout
- **Clear**: Captions are easy to read and never cover important visual information
- **Readable**: Captions appear long enough to be read (not rushed off-screen)
- **Equal**: All speakers and important sounds (music, applause, laughter) are captioned, not just the main speaker

A good test: Could a student who is deaf or hard of hearing learn from this video the same way a hearing student would, using only the captions? If yes, the captions are of sufficient quality.

contentBlocks.title: Auto-Captions Gone Wrong: Real Examples from Academic Contexts
contentBlocks.body:
Here are real (anonymized) examples of auto-caption failures in college courses:

**Example 1: Biology Lecture**

A biology professor recorded a 30-minute lecture on cell biology. YouTube auto-captions were turned on. Here is what happened:

Professor says: "Today we're discussing the structure and function of the mitochondria, often called the powerhouse of the cell."

Auto-captions generated: "Today we're discussing the structure and function of the middle chondria, often called the power house of the cell."

Then: "Note that the cristae increase the surface area for ATP synthesis."

Auto-captions: "Note that the christies increase the surface area for 80 P synthesis."

A student using these captions would struggle to understand the content. Even worse, they might study from the wrong terminology for the exam. The professor needed to manually edit all the biological vocabulary — a task that took about 90 minutes.

**Example 2: Spanish Language Course**

A Spanish instructor recorded a conversation between two native speakers for her intermediate Spanish course. She enabled Zoom auto-captions.

Native speaker 1 says in Spanish: "¿Cómo está tu familia?"

Zoom generated: "Como que está tufa milia?"

The captions are almost unreadable. The professor had to re-record the conversation with clearer audio, record it separately for editing, and use a professional caption service — a much more expensive solution than she initially anticipated.

**Example 3: Professor with Accent**

A professor from India recorded a lecture on abstract algebra. Her accent, while perfectly clear to native English speakers, confused the ASR algorithm:

She says: "Consider this set of permutations."

Auto-captions: "Consider this set of purr mutations."

She says: "The identity element is crucial."

Auto-captions: "The identity element is crucial." (This one was correct!)

But then: "We can define the group operation."

Auto-captions: "We can define the group operation." (Also correct.)

The pattern: Some utterances are fine; others are completely wrong. She manually reviewed and edited the captions, focusing on the math terminology and replacing "purr mutations" with "permutations."

**The Takeaway**: Auto-captions are a **starting point, not a solution**. Always review auto-generated captions and edit them, especially:
- The first minute of the video (to establish names and key terms)
- Any discipline-specific vocabulary
- Names of people and places
- The last minute (to catch any drift in accuracy)

Full review is ideal, but if time is limited, prioritizing these sections dramatically improves quality.

contentBlocks.title: Practical Captioning Workflow: Tools Faculty Can Use
contentBlocks.body:
You have several options for captioning faculty-created and assigned videos, depending on where the video is hosted and your time/budget constraints.

**Option 1: YouTube Studio (for your own recordings)**

If you upload videos to YouTube (private, unlisted, or public), YouTube's auto-caption feature is free and relatively easy:

1. Upload your video to YouTube
2. Go to YouTube Studio → Subtitles
3. Click on "Auto-generate" for your language (English, Spanish, etc.)
4. YouTube generates captions automatically (usually within an hour)
5. Review and edit the auto-generated captions in the editor
6. Publish the captions
7. Download the captions as an SRT or VTT file to archive or share

**Time commitment**: 10 minutes for auto-generation; 30–60 minutes for editing (depending on content and quality).

**Option 2: Otter.ai (for automatic transcription + captions)**

Otter.ai is an AI transcription service with a free tier:

1. Upload audio or video (or record live)
2. Otter generates a transcript and time-stamped captions automatically
3. Edit the transcript in Otter's interface (which is quite user-friendly)
4. Export as captions, transcript, or both
5. Import captions into your LMS or video platform

**Free tier**: 600 minutes per month (enough for a 15-week course with one 45-minute lecture per week).

**Paid tier**: More minutes and features like speaker identification and keyword highlighting.

**Time commitment**: 15 minutes for upload and generation; 20–45 minutes for editing.

**Option 3: Microsoft Stream (if your institution uses Office 365)**

Microsoft Stream (part of Microsoft Teams) has built-in auto-captioning:

1. Upload video to Stream
2. Stream auto-generates captions
3. Stream generates a transcript accessible alongside the video
4. Edit captions in Stream's editor
5. Students see captions automatically when they watch

**Advantage**: Integrated with your LMS if you use Canvas/Blackboard + Teams.

**Time commitment**: 5 minutes for upload; 20–40 minutes for editing.

**Option 4: Zoom (for live lectures)**

Zoom has built-in live auto-captions:

1. Enable "Live Transcript" in Zoom settings before the meeting
2. During the live session, captions appear on screen in real-time
3. After the meeting, download the transcript
4. Review and edit the transcript
5. Post the edited transcript alongside the recording

**Advantage**: No additional tool or cost; works for live and recorded content.

**Limitation**: Live captions are less accurate than post-recording captions (they are generated in real-time with no chance for the algorithm to learn context).

**Time commitment**: 0 minutes live (automatic); 30–60 minutes to review and clean up the transcript afterward.

**Option 5: Professional Captioning (for high-stakes or complex content)**

If your video is important for student learning, has heavy technical terminology, or is frequently reused, professional captioning might be worth the investment:

- **Rev, 3Play Media, Ai-Media**: Professional services ($1–$2+ per minute of video)
- **Your institution's Disability Services or Media Lab**: Many universities have in-house captioning services for faculty
- **Student assistants**: Some universities employ student workers to caption videos — ask your CTL or DS office

**Advantage**: High accuracy, quick turnaround, someone else does the work.

**Disadvantage**: Cost and advanced planning needed.

**Time commitment**: 10 minutes to upload and review the final product.

**Decision Tree for Choosing a Method**:

```
I recorded the video myself, and it's hosted on YouTube?
→ Use YouTube Studio's auto-captions + 30-45 min editing

I need transcripts and captions, and I'm okay with a small learning curve?
→ Use Otter.ai (free tier for most faculty)

I teach live Zoom sessions and want same-day accessibility?
→ Enable Zoom's live captions + download and review the transcript after

I use Microsoft Teams/Stream and want integrated video?
→ Upload to Stream and use its auto-captions

This is a required video for a high-enrollment course, and accuracy matters?
→ Budget for professional captioning or check with your library/DS office

I assigned a publisher video, and it doesn't have captions?
→ Contact the publisher and request captions; escalate to your accessibility office if they refuse
```

**Pro Tip**: Start with auto-captions, then edit. Do not try to manually caption from scratch (unless you speak shorthand). The auto-caption algorithm is right most of the time — you are just correcting the errors. This approach cuts editing time in half compared to transcribing by ear.

Captions and transcripts are legally required for pre-recorded video and audio content in courses. Auto-generated captions are a fast, free starting point but must be reviewed and edited for accuracy — especially technical terminology, proper nouns, and accented speech. Use free tools like YouTube Studio, Otter.ai, Microsoft Stream, or Zoom's built-in caption features. For complex content, professional captioning services are available through your institution or external vendors. The key: do not post auto-captions without editing them.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Do you use video or audio in your courses — your own recordings, YouTube, publisher content, or podcasts? Pick one video or audio file you assign or create. Check: (1) Does it currently have captions or a transcript? (2) If yes, evaluate the first 2 minutes for accuracy (do names, terminology, and dialogue match what you hear?). If no, note what tool you would use to add captions (YouTube, Otter.ai, etc.) and estimate how long the process would take. Report your findings.
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response describes a specific, real video or audio file the participant uses in their teaching. It shows that they actually checked for captions or listened to the first 2 minutes (not a hypothetical analysis). If captions exist, they assess accuracy. If not, they identify a specific tool and realistic time estimate.
